questions.question: Why does JSX use plain JavaScript expressions like ternaries and `&&` for branching instead of an `if` statement inside the markup?
options: JSX has no `if` statement, so branching must be done with expressions inside `{}`
`if` statements are slower than ternaries when React reconciles
React strips all `if` statements out of components at build time
`if` statements are only allowed inside hooks, not in the render body
questions.explanation: The reviewer states JSX has no `if` statement, so you branch with JavaScript expressions inside `{}` (early return, ternary, or `&&`). It is a syntactic fact about JSX, not a performance concern.

questions.question: When a component returns `null` to render nothing, the component still runs and its hooks still fire.
questions.explanation: The reviewer notes returning `null` (or `false`/`undefined`) renders nothing, but the component body still executes and its hooks still run — which is why the guard must come after all hooks.

questions.question: Given `count` is the number `0`, what does `{count && <Badge/>}` render to the DOM, and why?
options: The literal text `0`, because `&&` short-circuits and returns `0`, a falsy but renderable value
Nothing, because `0` is falsy and React always renders falsy values as nothing
The `<Badge/>` element, because `&&` coerces `0` to `true`
A runtime error, because `&&` cannot be used inside JSX braces
questions.explanation: `&&` short-circuits and returns its left operand `0` when falsy. React renders `null`/`undefined`/booleans as nothing, but a falsy number like `0` is a renderable value and shows as text. Guard with `count > 0 && ...`.

questions.question: Which of these values does React render as **nothing** (no visible output)? Select all that apply.
options: `true`
`null`
`undefined`
`"hello"`
questions.explanation: React renders `true`, `false`, `null`, and `undefined` as nothing. A falsy number like `0` renders as the text `0`, and a non-empty string like `"hello"` renders as text.

questions.question: Which expression safely renders `<Badge/>` only when `count` is positive, with no risk of leaking a stray `0` to the DOM?
options: `{count > 0 && <Badge/>}`
`{count && <Badge/>}`
`{count ? <Badge/> : 0}`
`{count || <Badge/>}`
questions.explanation: `count > 0` is a real boolean, so the left operand of `&&` can never be a renderable `0`. `{count && ...}` leaks `0`, and `{count || <Badge/>}` would render Badge precisely when count is 0.

questions.question: What is the scope of uniqueness required for a `key` on elements returned from a `.map`?
options: Unique among its siblings (the elements in that same list)
Globally unique across the entire application
Unique among all elements rendered by the same component
Unique per render, regenerated each time the list renders
questions.explanation: The reviewer states a key must be unique among its siblings, not globally. Keys are scoped to the list they belong to.

questions.question: Inside a child component rendered in a list, reading `props.key` returns the key value you passed from the parent.
questions.explanation: `key` is not a prop — React consumes it for reconciliation. Reading `props.key` inside the child is `undefined` (and triggers a warning). Pass the id separately if the child needs it.

questions.question: A list of editable rows uses `key={i}` (the array index). A new item is inserted at the **top** of the list. What is the observed bug?
options: The draft text and state stay attached to the slot/position, so the new top row shows the old first row's draft text
React throws a duplicate-key error and refuses to render the list
Every row unmounts and remounts, clearing all draft text across the whole list
The list renders correctly because indices are always stable and unique
questions.explanation: Index keys tie identity to position. On insert at the top, indices shift, so React keeps `key 0` mounted and only swaps its props — input values, focus, and state stick to the slot, not the item. There is no duplicate-key error since indices are still unique.

questions.question: For which lists is using the array index as the `key` an acceptable choice? Select all that apply.
options: A static, append-only list that is never reordered or filtered
A list the user can sort by column
A read-only list whose positions never change
A searchable/filterable list
A list with insertions and deletions in the middle
questions.explanation: Index as key is safe only when positions never change: static, append-only, read-only. Sorting, filtering/searching, and mid-list insert/delete all shift positions, so identity must follow the item via a stable id.

questions.question: You want to deliberately reset a child component's internal state when a particular value changes. What does changing that component's `key` do, and why does it work?
options: A changed key makes React unmount the old node and mount a fresh one, discarding the old state
A changed key forces React to re-run the child's render without touching its state
A changed key tells React to merge the new props into the existing state
A changed key has no effect on a single (non-list) element
questions.explanation: Keys drive reconciliation: a matching key updates/moves the existing node and keeps state, while a changed key unmounts the old node and mounts a fresh one — a deliberate way to reset a component's state. This works on any element, not just lists.

questions.question: What happens when you write `onClick={handleClick()}` instead of `onClick={handleClick}`?
options: `handleClick` is called during render and its return value is used as the handler
React debounces the handler so it only fires once per render
The handler is correctly bound and fires on click, just with extra parentheses
React throws a compile error because handlers must be arrow functions
questions.explanation: `handleClick()` calls the function during render and passes its return value as the handler. This is a common cause of 'fires immediately / infinite re-render' bugs. You must pass the reference `handleClick`.

questions.question: What is the idiomatic way to pass an argument to an event handler, e.g. deleting a specific row by `id`?
options: `onClick={() => deleteRow(id)}` — an inline arrow defers the call to click time
`onClick={deleteRow(id)}` — call it directly with the argument
`onClick={deleteRow.bind(id)}` — bind the argument as `this`
`onClick={deleteRow, id}` — pass the function and argument as a pair
questions.explanation: An inline arrow `() => deleteRow(id)` defers the call until click time while letting you pass arguments. `deleteRow(id)` would call it during render; `bind(id)` passes id as `this`, not the first argument.

questions.question: By the naming convention, a parent owns a play behavior and hands it to a child `Toolbar`. How should the prop and the parent's function be named?
options: Child prop `onPlay`, parent function `handlePlay`
Child prop `handlePlay`, parent function `onPlay`
Child prop `playHandler`, parent function `onPlayClick`
Both named `onPlay` to keep them in sync
questions.explanation: Built-in and custom event-exposing props use the `onX` prefix (`onPlay`), while your own handler functions use the `handleX` prefix (`handlePlay`). The parent passes `handlePlay` into the child's `onPlay` prop.

questions.question: Like DOM events, React's synthetic events bubble: an event fires on the deepest element first, then travels up through each ancestor's handler of the same type.
questions.explanation: React wraps native events in a SyntheticEvent and, like DOM events, they bubble from the target up through ancestor handlers of the same type.

questions.question: Which statements about `e.stopPropagation()` and `e.preventDefault()` are correct? Select all that apply.
options: `e.stopPropagation()` stops the event from climbing to ancestor handlers
`e.preventDefault()` cancels the browser's default action (e.g. form submit, link navigation)
`e.stopPropagation()` also cancels the browser's default action
`e.preventDefault()` also stops the event from bubbling
The two are independent — one does not imply the other
questions.explanation: `stopPropagation` halts bubbling but not the default action; `preventDefault` cancels the default action but not bubbling. The two effects are independent, so the cross-claims (3 and 4) are wrong.

questions.question: How does `onClickCapture` differ from `onClick`, and what is it useful for?
options: It runs top-down from the root toward the target, before the bubbling pass — useful for routers/analytics that must see every click first
It runs only after `onClick`, capturing clicks the bubble phase missed
It captures the click and prevents `onClick` from ever firing on that element
It runs bottom-up like `onClick` but on a separate, isolated event queue
questions.explanation: Capture-phase handlers run top-down from the root toward the target, before the bubbling pass runs back up. This lets global intercepts like routers or analytics see every click first.

questions.question: Where does React attach its single delegated listener per event type, and what changed in React 17?
options: At the root container passed to `createRoot`; before React 17 it attached to `document`
At the `document` node; React 17 moved it to `window`
Directly on each element with a handler; React 17 switched to one root listener
At the `body` element in all versions; React 17 only changed pooling
questions.explanation: React attaches one listener per event type at the root container (the `createRoot` DOM node), then dispatches synthetic events. Before React 17 it attached to `document` — relevant when nesting multiple roots or mixing with non-React code.

questions.question: After React 17 removed event pooling, you can read properties off the synthetic event `e` in async code without calling `e.persist()`.
questions.explanation: Pooling was removed in React 17, so the synthetic event is no longer reused. You can safely read `e` in async callbacks without `e.persist()`.
